title: `podman run alpine /bin/ls` fails with Error: crun: open `/home/u1/.local/share/containers/storage/overlay/123/merged`: Permission denied: OCI permission denied
body:
### Issue Description

When i run `podman run alpine /bin/ls`

I got error 
```
Error: crun: open `/home/u1/.local/share/containers/storage/overlay/41bfd118199856f58b90b8a47f7e69b3821dd492e1379782cc0218004d1cce23/merged`: Permission denied: OCI permission denied
```

Same command with loglevel debug:
```

Error: crun: open `/home/u1/.local/share/containers/storage/overlay/cb4fc0064055dc671c247f0265806a3cd5c12580c8f4952a1e5028e2762c1e77/merged`: Permission denied: OCI permission denied
DEBU[0001] Shutting down engines
```

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Install podman 5.3.1
1. run `podman run alpine /bin/ls`

### Describe the results you received

Error: crun: open `/home/u1/.local/share/containers/storage/overlay/41bfd118199856f58b90b8a47f7e69b3821dd492e1379782cc0218004d1cce23/merged`: Permission denied: OCI permission denied

### Describe the results you expected

Run ls in container - print list of directories in the container root directory

comments.body:
can you run it as root I can help you just recently got it working myself.

what does  mount | grep cgroup       say

> can you run it as root I can help you just recently got it working myself.

it works:
```
>sudo podman run alpine ls
bin
dev
etc
home
lib
media
mnt
opt
proc
root
run
sbin
srv
sys
tmp
usr
var
```

> what does mount | grep cgroup say

```
> mount | grep cgroup
cgroup2 on /sys/fs/cgroup type cgroup2 (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,nsdelegate,memory_recursiveprot)
```

crun is the what uses systemd or the oci which is what youll be needing to use.

default config for podman is  #cgroupfs disabled 

systemd  is handling the cgroup

overlay     <---- default as overlay2 is interpreted as being included in overlay

sudo su
podman images

get the container id

podman image rm -f id

we have to remove the image because if its created by root you may have a problem accessing it as user

or you can use  podman system restart           (deleted all images containers etc)


get around the namespace issues. this will work for user on your system automatically

rm /etc/subuid
rm /etc/subgid

sudo sh -c "echo $(id -un):100000:65536 >> /etc/subuid"            #this works copy paste to automatically add your user
sudo sh -c "echo $(id -un):100000:65536 >> /etc/subgid"           

podman system migrate 



ill dig up a config file for storage and containers for you we will set them to default. ill check if your system uses systemd which im sure it does. ill get back to you

podman images

get id

podman image inspect --format "user: {{.User}}" alpine          #or id

this will say  either    user:        or   user:     some group             may give you an idea of the needed permissions



there are also binaries in /usr/bin        newgidmap and newuidmap       im not sure how to use those but may help in getting around the permissions.



Checked your setup and it looks ok     my ubuntu is using libpod.conf in /etc/containers/ its pretty basic. so a containers.conf and storage.conf will override any libpod.conf        so you can further specify settings with those files.

Ill grab the default configs for arch linux and then mine (using slackware) and send those to you

get a default containers.conf

add
userns = "keep-id"
cgroup_manager = "systemd"


then a default storage.conf

storage.conf

[storage]

// Default Storage Driver, Must be set for proper operation.
driver = "overlay"

[storage.options.overlay]

ignore_chown_errors = "true"

// mountopt specifies comma separated list of extra mount options
mountopt = "nodev"




other than that there is probably a command podman --privileged    or a systemd component through rootless mode. its probably something very simple just got to find it.

it could even be your boot parameter   cgroup_no_v1=all  

podman will not work in rootless mode with cgroup v2 if any cgroup v1 is enabled this is called a hierarchy or hybrid mode. podman only supports that with root.  root works with v1 and v2 and rootless only v2.

so by passing the boot parameter we enable true cgroup v2 mode and podman will work in rootless with v2. its a security thing as they are adopting v2 and phasing out v1.

I have not changed anything, and rebooted my pc, and the issue went away.
Closing this issue for now. If i will encounter it again, i will reopen.
